questionText: The weights, $X$ kg, of adult male cats of a certain breed are modelled by $X \sim N(4.5, 0.6^2)$. The central $80\%$ of weights lie between $p$ kg and $q$ kg, symmetrically about the mean. Find $p$ and $q$, giving your answers to $3$ significant figures.

steps.explanation: The weights are normal with mean $4.5$ kg and standard deviation $0.6$ kg. We want the two weights that enclose the middle $80\%$ of cats symmetrically about the mean.
The central $80\%$ is the region under the bell curve, centred on the mean, that holds $80\%$ of the area, so the interval probability is $0.80$.

steps.description: Split the remaining 20% between the tails
steps.explanation: The $20\%$ not in the middle is shared equally between the two tails, giving $10\%$ in each by symmetry.
The lower boundary $p$ has exactly $10\%$ of the distribution below it.
The upper boundary $q$ leaves $10\%$ in the upper tail, so $90\%$ lies below it.
The inverse normal for a cumulative probability of $0.10$ is negative, since the $10$th percentile lies to the left of the mean.
By symmetry the $90$th percentile is the mirror image, giving $z_q = +1.282$.
Standardising $p$ must reproduce the lower $z$-value, giving an equation we can solve for $p$.
Multiplying by $\sigma = 0.6$ and adding the mean converts the $z$-value back to a weight in kilograms.
This is the weight below which only $10\%$ of the cats fall.
The upper boundary satisfies the mirror equation with $z = +1.282$.
The same rearrangement gives the upper weight in kilograms.
This is the weight that only $10\%$ of the cats exceed.
The midpoint of the interval equals the mean $4.5$ kg, and each endpoint is $0.769$ kg from it, confirming the interval is correctly centred.
